problem_summary: 2018 MCM Problem C (data-insight). Four US-Mexico border states (CA, AZ, NM, TX) want a realistic interstate energy compact to increase use of cleaner/renewable energy. Given ~50 years of data across 605 variables per state, the team had to: (1) build an energy profile for each state, (2) characterize/rank profiles, (3) predict future energy profiles, and (4) set renewable-energy usage targets for 2025 and 2050 plus actionable policies to reach them. This paper's distinctive move is reframing the data problem through an economics lens, treating energy adoption as a macroeconomic equilibrium driven by demand/supply and policy shocks.
models_used: Cointegration analysis with Augmented Dickey-Fuller (ADF) unit-root test for variable de-correlation/screening (p<0.05 pairs treated as cointegrated, removed redundancy from 605 vars down to ~10 categories)
Multidimensional Scaling (MDS) to further reduce/visualize variable similarity from the distance matrix
Mean Substitution for missing-data imputation (with an explicit zero-vs-missing disambiguation step)
Revised EROI (Energy Return On Investment) evaluation system — a custom index adapting financial ROI, splitting investment into internal cost (EIC) and external expenditure (EEE)
Baseline multiple linear regression on 43 energy factors (deliberately presented, then rejected as inadequate)

Dynamic New Keynesian IS-LM model (3 coupled equations: IS curve + two LM curves / a Taylor-rule interest-rate equation) with forward-looking rational-expectation terms approximated by a 5-lag weighted moving average
Enhanced NK IS-LM model with additive demand shocks (nu_t) and supply shocks (mu_t) to simulate policy interventions
Sensitivity analysis sweeping IS-LM parameters (e.g. theta_x over [-1,1]) on a representative series (Arizona natural gas)
key_techniques: EROI re-engineering: adapted financial ROI into energy terms, then monetized externalities — pollution external cost per energy type (coal 20.4 down to wind 0.54 $/MBtu from OECD/EEA/Pew sources) folded into the denominator as EEE

Normalization for confounders: energy-intensity factor I = GDP(2005)/CurrentGDP to strip inflation, plus a regional price quality factor alpha_i = P_i/P_1 to remove the 'Texas has cheap oil because it has oil' distortion
Sector influence weights (Residential 1.2, Commercial 1.1, Industrial 0.8, Transportation 1.3) to weight pollution by where it occurs
Separate End-use EROI vs Electric-power-plant EROI formulas combined via PEE (share consumed by electric plants) into a single Total EROI
Honest model laddering: showing the simple linear regression failing (overfitting, can't follow policy-driven trend breaks) to motivate the economic model
Justifying the absence of ARMA/GARCH by citing failed ADF stationarity tests — turning a non-result into a defensible modeling decision
Policy simulation via shock injection: demand subsidies, supply taxes, interest-rate cuts, and short- vs long-term (1-yr vs 3-yr) shocks compared quantitatively (3-yr shock ~200% more solar uptake)
Forward-looking expectations operationalized as a fixed-weight moving average [0.5,0.15,0.1,0.1,0.1,0.05]
why_it_won: From a judge's perspective, this paper won by giving a genuinely original interpretive frame to a generic 'crunch the spreadsheet' data problem. Instead of yet another regression/time-series forecast, it cast renewable-energy adoption as a macroeconomic system, which let it do the one thing the prompt actually demanded but most teams underdeliver: directly model HOW policies achieve the targets (subsidies, taxes, interest rates, cross-state cooperation) as quantifiable supply/demand shocks. The custom EROI index gave a defensible, economically-meaningful ranking criterion rather than an arbitrary score. It demonstrates mathematical maturity by deriving a result (4-model ladder), explicitly rejecting tempting-but-wrong tools (ARMA/GARCH via ADF, plain linear regression via overfitting), and every parameter carries an economic interpretation so the model is interpretable, not a black box. The deliverables map cleanly onto the ask — concrete 2025/2050 percentage targets per state plus a governor's memo and a formal compact resolution — which judges reward heavily on a policy-flavored C problem.
section_highlights: Structure mirrors the task list exactly: Data Processing -> Energy Profile -> EROI Evaluation -> Predictive Modeling -> Sensitivity -> Strengths/Weaknesses -> Conclusions, then a Memo and Appendix code. The summary sheet front-loads the whole pipeline and the headline numbers (CA ~42% renewable by 2025; all states 38-51% by 2050). The EROI section is the analytical centerpiece, with every revision (US-dollar adjustment, regional adjustment, externality cost table, sector weights) building to the three combined formulas. The Predictive Modeling section's rhetorical strength is the model ladder: linear regression shown failing -> dynamic NK IS-LM -> shock-augmented version, each transition motivated by a concrete shortcoming. The policy subsection is unusually concrete for an MCM paper, with four labeled policy levers each tied to a figure. It closes the loop by feeding the 2025/2050 targets back into the EROI model to report future EROI. Communication artifacts (governor memo + WHEREAS/THEREFORE compact resolution) show audience awareness.
pitfalls_or_limitations: Many parameters lack reported fitted values or fitting procedure — phi, lambda, gamma, theta_x, theta_pi are said to be 'set to fit historical data' but the calibration method (least squares? grid search?) and resulting values are never shown, so results are not reproducible

The forward-looking expectation is faked: rational expectations are reduced to a hard-coded 5-lag weighted moving average, and the weight vector [0.5,0.15,...] is asserted without justification or sensitivity testing
EROI external-cost and sector-influence numbers (e.g. sector weights 1.2/1.1/0.8/1.3) appear hand-set or loosely sourced; the influence weights have no cited basis

Several appendix/figure data tables are clearly garbled or placeholder (Figure 10 all-zeros, Figure 14 nonsense digit strings, far-future years to 2950), suggesting weak figure-data hygiene that survived into the final paper

Assumptions are aggressive: inflation/GDP deflator constant forever, resources never exhausted before 2050, all post-2009 energy identical in total cost — these undercut the economic realism the model otherwise leans on

The IS-LM model 'does not always have equilibrium' (admitted in sensitivity section) and ignores technology and natural-resource constraints, so long-horizon projections (and the 2950 extrapolations) are not trustworthy
Cointegration is used somewhat unconventionally as a generic correlation/redundancy filter rather than for its standard long-run-equilibrium purpose
Sensitivity analysis is thin: one parameter on one state's one series, not a systematic perturbation of the targets or rankings
reusable_patterns: Reframe a 'data' problem with a domain theory (here: macroeconomics) so you can model mechanisms and policy levers, not just fit curves — this is what separates an Outstanding C paper from a competent forecast
Build a custom, named evaluation index (EROI-style) by adapting a well-known metric (ROI) and explicitly itemizing each correction term — gives judges a defensible single number to rank entities
Monetize externalities / fold qualitative concerns (pollution, sector context) into the objective via cited cost tables and weight vectors
The model ladder: present a simple baseline, show concretely why it fails, then introduce the sophisticated model — demonstrates judgment and earns the complexity
Normalize away confounders early and name them (inflation via a deflator ratio, regional price advantage via a quality factor) so cross-entity comparison is fair
Justify what you DON'T use: cite a statistical test (ADF stationarity) to defend omitting standard tools (ARMA/GARCH) — pre-empts judge objections
Treat policies as additive shocks to a dynamic system so you can quantitatively compare intervention designs (demand vs supply, short vs long term)
Close the loop: feed your predicted targets back through your evaluation model to report the resulting score, showing internal consistency
Package deliverables for the stated audience: a plain-language governor memo plus a formal WHEREAS/THEREFORE compact resolution alongside the technical body
